learning.summary: Linux is a major operating system family known for its open-source development model, flexibility, and broad use across desktops, servers, embedded systems, and cloud environments. It is distributed through many different Linux distributions.
learning.explanation:
Linux is an operating system built around an open-source development model, allowing its source code to be viewed, modified, and improved by a global community of developers. Many Linux distributions are available, each packaging the Linux kernel with different software, desktop environments, and management tools.

A significant advantage of Linux is that many distributions are available at no cost, making Linux popular for education, servers, development, and enterprise deployments. Organizations can choose distributions that best match their support and stability requirements.

Hardware compatibility and vendor support can vary depending on the distribution and device. Some hardware manufacturers provide official Linux drivers, while others primarily target Windows, requiring community-developed drivers or additional configuration.

Understanding the Linux kernel, distributions, package management, and administration tools provides the foundation for more advanced Linux topics covered later in the curriculum.

facts.text: Linux is an open-source operating system family.
Linux is available through many different distributions.
Many Linux distributions are available at no cost.
Hardware compatibility depends on available driver support.

examples.text: An organization deploys Ubuntu Linux on development workstations because it is free, well-supported, and has a large software repository.

examTips: Know that Linux is open source and distributed through multiple distributions.
commonMistakes: Assuming every Linux distribution includes the same software or hardware support.
scenarios: Recommend Linux for an environment that values flexibility and low licensing costs.
pbqIdeas: Match common Linux characteristics with deployment scenarios.

reviewNotes: Hardware support discussion integrates the merged linux.hardware-support concept.
Kernel prerequisite reference added as contextual enrichment beyond the directly supported evidence.
